chunk_summary: Risk-stratified follow-up for MAM involves baseline assessment, risk-based scheduling, and response evaluation to ensure effective outpatient care and prevent deterioration.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of baseline anthropometric and clinical assessments in MAM follow-up.
Learn to classify children into high-risk and standard-risk categories using WHO criteria.
Develop skills to assess response trajectories and adjust follow-up plans accordingly.
Recognize common programmatic failure points and strategies to address them.
key_facts: Baseline assessment includes weight, height/length, oedema, and MUAC or WHZ/WLZ.
WHO risk criteria include MUAC 115–119 mm, WAZ < -3, age < 24 months, relapse, comorbidity, or severe social adversity.
High-risk children should be reviewed within 7 days, while standard-risk children within 14 days.
Follow-up focuses on clinical well-being, dietary intake, and anthropometric improvement.
State models often recommend weekly or fortnightly visits during an 8-week active program period.
reference_quote: Use WHO risk criteria (for example MUAC 115–119 mm, WAZ < -3, age <24 months, relapse, comorbidity, or severe social adversity) to classify the child as high-risk versus standard-risk.
